record_office_terminology.land_records: Clerk of Circuit Court (deeds, land records, chancery)
record_office_terminology.probate: Clerk of Circuit Court (wills, estate accounts, inventories)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Circuit Court (marriage bonds from colonial era; statewide registration 1853)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1912 (births/deaths statewide registration; counties/cities may have earlier records; delayed birth certificates common)

burned_county_authority: Library of Virginia, Lost Records Localities; Minor T. Weisiger, 'Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm'; Daphne S. Gentry, 'Virginia Supreme Court of Appeals Records' (for reconstructed land records); FamilySearch Research Wiki — Virginia Burned Counties
administrative_notes: Virginia is one of the 13 original colonies (Jamestown 1607, first permanent English settlement). It has 95 counties and 38 independent cities — the most independent cities of any US state. Independent cities are separate FIPS jurisdictions, not part of any county. The original 8 shires (1634) became counties: Accomack (Northampton), Charles City, Elizabeth City (now Hampton), Henrico, Isle of Wight, James City, Upper Norfolk (extinct), Warwick (now Newport News). Three counties were dissolved: Elizabeth City County (merged into Hampton 1908), Nansemond County (merged into Suffolk 1974), and Warwick County (merged into Newport News 1958). Princess Anne County merged into Virginia Beach 1963 and Norfolk County merged into Chesapeake 1963. Two colonial counties — Old Rappahannock (split into Essex and Richmond 1692) and Fincastle (divided 1777) — are extinct. The Library of Virginia (LVA) in Richmond is the primary state repository with microfilmed records for virtually all jurisdictions.

core_repositories.description: Virginia's state library, archives, and records center. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, wills, tax lists, court orders), colonial records, state government records, military records, vital records, newspapers, and maps. The LVA Chancery Records Index is a groundbreaking digitized collection of chancery court cases searchable by name. Virginia Memory digital collections provide online access to many records. Land Office patents and grants database available online.

core_repositories.source_name: Virginia Museum of History & Culture (Virginia Historical Society)
core_repositories.repository: Virginia Museum of History & Culture, Richmond
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository with family papers, plantation records, business records, and organizational archives. Extensive collection of Virginia genealogical and historical periodicals. Lee Family papers, Carter Family papers, and other elite Virginia family collections.

core_repositories.repository: John D. Rockefeller Jr. Library, Williamsburg
core_repositories.description: Premier colonial Virginia research center. Holds York County records (1633+), microfilmed colonial county records, Virginia Gazette newspaper, colonial government records, and extensive secondary sources. Essential for 17th-18th century Virginia research.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Virginia Special Collections (Albert and Shirley Small Library)
core_repositories.description: Extensive manuscript collections including family papers, plantation records, Civil War letters, and university records. Holdings span the colonial era to present. Strong in Piedmont and central Virginia family collections. Digital collections available online.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court orders), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Extensive microfilm partnership with LVA provides digitized county records for virtually all Virginia jurisdictions.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes independent city pages.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Virginia newspapers from the 1730s forward. Includes Virginia Gazette (colonial era, one of America's earliest newspapers), Richmond Enquirer, Norfolk papers, and county weeklies across the state.

LVA's digitized newspaper collection covering Virginia newspapers not in Chronicling America. Includes county weeklies, city dailies, and specialized publications documenting legal notices, estates, and community events.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: LVA — Enslaved Virginians and Free People of Color
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Personal property tax lists (1782-1865) naming enslaved by slaveholder, free negro registers (county-level from 1793), cohabitation records (1866), and estate inventories listing enslaved by name. LVA's online guides to African American sources are essential starting points.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Virginia (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and complaint files. Many indexed on FamilySearch. NARA microfilm M1048, M1053.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Black History Museum and Cultural Center of Virginia
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Archives and collections documenting African American life in Virginia from the colonial era to present. Manuscripts, photographs, oral histories, and genealogical resources focused on Virginia's Black communities.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Virginia Indian Records — LVA and NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of Virginia's state-recognized tribes (Pamunkey, Mattaponi, Rappahannock, Nansemond, Monacan, Upper Mattaponi, Chickahominy). Colonial treaty records, reservation records, and Walter Plecker's Bureau of Vital Statistics records (1912-1946, significant for racial integrity act documentation). Virginia Indian Heritage Foundation resources.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Jamestown Colony and Powhatan Confederacy Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Library of Virginia / Colonial Williamsburg / Smithsonian NMAI
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Colonial records documenting interactions with the Powhatan Confederacy and other Virginia tribes. Land patents on former tribal lands, treaty documents, and colonial court records mentioning Native peoples. Archaeological records at Jamestown Rediscovery and Virginia Department of Historic Resources.

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications (1888-1934), Confederate service records, militia records (colonial through antebellum), Revolutionary War records (bounty land warrants, pension applications), War of 1812 records, and WWI/WWII draft registration cards. LVA's Confederate pension files are among the most detailed in any Southern state — include family information, residence history, and unit detail.
Honor roll databases for Virginians who died in service from WWI through present. Educational archives and oral history collections.

land_and_property.source_name: Virginia Land Office Patents and Grants
land_and_property.description: Colonial and state land patents from 1623 forward. Virginia used the headright system (1618-1705) — 50 acres per person transported to the colony. Northern Neck Proprietary (Fairfax Grant) covered 5.3 million acres between the Potomac and Rappahannock rivers — separate land office records at LVA. Treasury land warrants continued through the 19th century.

The Northern Neck Proprietary (Lord Fairfax Grant) was a 5.3 million acre proprietary covering the area between the Potomac and Rappahannock rivers. Separate land grant records from 1690-1862 at LVA. Covers Fairfax, Prince William, Stafford, King George, Westmoreland, Northumberland, Lancaster, Richmond County, Loudoun, Fauquier, Culpeper, Rappahannock, Clarke, Warren, Shenandoah, Frederick, and parts of other counties.

vital_records.source_name: Virginia Department of Health — Division of Vital Records
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1912 (some earlier city registrations — Richmond, Norfolk, others). Marriage records from 1853 (county-level bonds and registers often earlier). Historical records (75+ years for births, 25+ for deaths) transferred to LVA. Pre-1912 vital events must be found in church records, county records, or newspaper notices.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Library of Virginia, Richmond / Virginia Historical Society
church_and_cemetery.description: Vestry books and parish registers from the established Church of England (later Episcopal). Key colonial parishes: Bruton (James City/York), St. Peter's (New Kent), Christ Church (Lancaster/Middlesex), Bristol (Prince George), Henrico, Elizabeth City. Parish registers often predate county records. William Meade's 'Old Churches, Ministers and Families of Virginia' (1857) is a key reference.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Virginia. Coverage varies by county — older Tidewater cemeteries often have extensive transcriptions. Family cemeteries on former plantation lands are important for African American genealogy. Check Virginia Department of Historic Resources for recorded cemetery locations.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Virginia Genealogical Society Magazine (formerly Quarterly). Maintains research resources and sponsors lectures and workshops.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, and research guides for all 95 Virginia counties and 38 independent cities.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Virginia has several extinct counties: Elizabeth City County (dissolved 1908, merged into Hampton — records at LVA and Hampton), Warwick County (dissolved 1958, merged into Newport News — records at LVA and Newport News), Nansemond County (dissolved 1974, merged into Suffolk — records at LVA and Suffolk), Princess Anne County (dissolved 1963, merged into Virginia Beach — records at LVA and Virginia Beach), Norfolk County (dissolved 1963, merged into Chesapeake — records at LVA and Chesapeake). Colonial-era extinct counties: Old Rappahannock County (divided 1692 into Essex and Richmond County — records at LVA), Fincastle County (divided 1777 into Montgomery, Washington, and Kentucky — records at LVA), and Charles River County (renamed York 1643). The original 8 shires of 1634 are the starting point for all Virginia county formation.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Virginia's courthouse record losses are primarily from Civil War damage and occasional fires. The most devastating losses occurred in Tidewater and Piedmont counties along major military campaign routes: King and Queen County (1828 fire + 1864 Civil War — nearly total loss), New Kent County (1787 fire + 1862 Civil War — nearly total loss), Gloucester County (1820 fire + 1865 damage), Hanover County (1865 fire), Stafford County (1841 fire), Caroline County (1836 fire), and Buckingham County (1869 fire). For burned counties, the Library of Virginia maintains a 'Lost Records Localities' guide identifying surviving sources. Tax lists, parish registers, and land office patents at LVA often provide substitute documentation. The LVA Chancery Records Index is especially valuable because chancery cases often reference destroyed records.
